main_sections.title: Hekko
main_sections.prose_summary: Chapter 9 is an informational text about Hekko, a traditional folk game from the Mokokchung district of Nagaland. The chapter opens by unpacking the word: 'Hek' means to challenge and 'ko' means tiger, so 'Hekko' means 'Challenging the tiger'. A brief folk story explains the game's origin — a mythical disagreement between Aami (humans) and Aakho (tigers) about who was superior, leading to the challenge that became the game. In the modern form, two teams of 11 players each play on a circle drawn on the ground. A toss decides which team is Aami (stays inside the circle forming a human chain) and which is Aakho (stays outside). Two Aakho players enter the circle at a time and try to push Aami players out; an Aami player pushed out is eliminated. The Aami team can catch Aakho players by surrounding them; a caught Aakho player must say 'Joko' (surrender) to be released, after which another Aakho player enters. Points are scored whenever a player from the opposing team is eliminated. The game ends when all Aakho players are caught, all Aami players are pushed out, or time runs out — the team with the most points wins. Special titles are awarded: the fastest Aami player becomes 'Khozuba' and the strongest Aakho player becomes 'Khotiba'. The chapter promotes awareness of India's regional traditional sports and implicitly celebrates physical strength, strategy, and cultural heritage.

reading.section_name: Let us Read + Let us Think A + Let us Think B

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us Read: Read the informational text 'Hekko' (pp. 89–92). New Words: declared, superiority, disagreement, surrender.

Let us Think A. Answer the following.
1. What does the word 'Hekko' mean?
2. What are the two teams in the game called?
3. How many players are there in each team?
4. What do the players in the Aami team do inside the circle?
5. Why do you think the Aakho team (Tiger) must say 'Joko' when they are caught?

question_bank.stem: What does the word 'Hekko' mean?
What are the two teams in the game called?
How many players are there in each team?
What do the players in the Aami team do inside the circle?
Why do you think the Aakho team (Tiger) must say 'Joko' when they are caught?

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Let us Think B. Think and discuss.
1. If you were playing the game Hekko, would you prefer to be in the Aami or Aakho team? Why?
2. If you could add a new rule to the game, what would it be? What would be the benefit of the new rule?

question_bank.stem: If you were playing the game Hekko, would you prefer to be in the Aami or Aakho team? Why?
If you could add a new rule to the game, what would it be? What would be the benefit of the new rule?

oracy.section_name: Let us Listen + Let us Speak A

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Let us Listen: Listen to the description of some traditional games of India. Identify each game and write its name in the space given below.
1. In this game, we stack seven stones on top of each other. The first team throws a ball to knock them down. After the stones are knocked down, the members of the first team hurriedly rearrange the stack while the opponent team catches the ball to quickly hit the members of the first team before the stones are restacked. If you are hit by the ball, you are out. Can you guess the name of the game?
2. In this game, there are two teams. One player from the first team crosses into the second team's side while chanting the name of the game continuously without taking a breath! The goal is to tag as many players as possible and return to their side before being caught. If the player is caught, the other team scores. Can you guess the name of this game?
3. This game is played with two wooden sticks — a short one and a longer one. The goal is to hit the shorter stick and make it jump in the air, then strike it again to send it flying. Players measure how far the shorter stick travels to score points.
4. In this game, two children form an arch with their arms while others walk under it, singing a song. When the song ends, the arch drops, and the player caught joins in. It's a fun and rhythmic game played in many parts of India.
5. In this game, children use a small round shaped glass object, aiming to knock out other glass round objects placed in a circle. Players flick the glass object with their fingers, trying to hit the target objects and win them. This is a popular outdoor game played in many parts of India.

question_bank.stem: Identify the traditional game described: seven stones stacked, a ball is used to knock them down and the opposing team tries to restake while the other team throws the ball at players. Name the game.
Identify the traditional game described: a player from one team crosses to the other side, chanting the game name without breath, tagging players and returning. Name the game.
Identify the traditional game described: two wooden sticks, a short and a longer one; hit the short stick to make it jump, then strike it to send it flying; score by distance. Name the game.
Identify the traditional game described: two children form an arch, others pass under singing, when the song ends the arch drops trapping a player. Name the game.
Identify the traditional game described: small round glass objects; players flick one to knock others placed in a circle. Name the game.

tasks_verbatim.transcript_text: 1. Seven stones stacked; first team knocks them down with a ball; that team must rearrange the stack while the opponent tries to hit them with the ball; if hit, you are out. 2. Two teams; one player crosses to the other side chanting the game name without breath, tags players, tries to return before being caught; other team scores if player is caught. 3. Two wooden sticks — short and long; hit the short stick to make it jump, then strike it to send it flying; score by distance. 4. Two children form an arch; others walk under singing; arch drops when song ends trapping a player who then joins. 5. Small round glass objects; flick one to knock others in a circle; win the objects you knock out.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us Speak A: Look at the pictures and identify the games being played. Have you played any of these games before? Discuss with your partner about the games you recognise. Which one is your favourite? Why do you enjoy playing it?

question_bank.stem: Look at the pictures. Have you played any of these games before? Discuss with your partner: which games do you recognise and which is your favourite? Why?

visual_stimulus.brief_description: Six illustrations showing different traditional Indian games being played by children (e.g. skipping, kabbadi, marbles, etc.)

word_work.section_name: Let us Learn A + Let us Learn B + Let us Learn C + Let us Learn D + Let us Learn E

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Let us Learn A: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence (homophones).
1. their/there — ………....…… is a beautiful park in my neighbourhood. / They completed ………....…… work very fast.
2. two/too — ………....…… of my friends participated in the poster-making competition. / I was ………....…… tired after playing.
3. through/threw — The bowler ………....…… the ball towards the batsman. / I can see the clouds ………....…… my window pane.
4. caught/cot — My mother ………....…… me eating an ice-cream at night. / I sleep on a ………....…… when I am at my grandmother's village.
5. right/write — While crossing the road, look to your ………....……, then left, then right again and then cross the road. / ………....…… in a neat and legible handwriting.

question_bank.stem: Choose: ………....…… (their/there) is a beautiful park in my neighbourhood.
Choose: They completed ………....…… (there/their) work very fast.
Choose: ………....…… (two/too) of my friends participated in the poster-making competition.
Choose: I was ………....…… (too/two) tired after playing.
Choose: The bowler ………....…… (through/threw) the ball towards the batsman.
Choose: I can see the clouds ………....…… (through/threw) my window pane.
Choose: My mother ………....…… (caught/cot) me eating an ice-cream at night.
Choose: I sleep on a ………....…… (caught/cot) when I am at my grandmother's village.
Choose: While crossing the road, look to your ………....…… (right/write), then left, then right again.
Choose: ………....…… (right/write) in a neat and legible handwriting.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us Learn B: Look at the pictures and write their names. One has been done for you (Stairs — 'st' blend). Complete the blend families for 'st', 'ch', and 'gl'.

question_bank.stem: Look at the pictures and write the name of each item. One is done for you (Stairs — 'st' blend). Complete the names for 'st', 'ch', and 'gl' blend words shown in pictures.

visual_stimulus.brief_description: Nine small illustrations arranged in three columns under blends 'st', 'ch', 'gl' — each picture shows an object whose name begins with that blend (e.g. stairs, chain, gloves)

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Let us Learn C: Match the animals with their sound.
I: 1. Tiger  2. Horse  3. Elephant  4. Dove  5. Goat  6. Monkey
II: coos / trumpets / roars / neighs / whoops / bleats

question_bank.stem: Match the animal to its sound: Tiger / Horse / Elephant / Dove / Goat / Monkey → coos / trumpets / roars / neighs / whoops / bleats

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us Learn D: Read the following sentences. Notice the underlined words (lived, declared, challenged). These words tell us events happened in the past. In past simple tense, you add -ed to verbs. There are also irregular verbs (came, drank, drove, saw, etc.) to which we don't add -ed.

question_bank.stem: Read the sentences with underlined past-tense verbs (lived, declared, challenged). Understand that -ed is added to regular verbs for past tense; irregular verbs have different forms (came, drank, drove, saw).

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Let us Learn E: Fill in the blanks using -ed.
Last week, we ……................... (visit) the park for a while. My mother ……...................   (point) at the children playing kabbadi. On our way home, my sister ……................... (jump) in a puddle and muddied everyone's clothes. I ……................... (enjoy) my visit to the park. My sister ……................... (want) to go to the pond but I ……................... (decide) to stay and watch the game of kabbadi. I ……................... (watch) the game for a while and then ……................... (join) my mother and sister. I ……................... (spot) a goldfish and my sister ……................... (notice) two tadpoles in the pond.

question_bank.stem: Fill in the blank with the past tense (-ed form): we ……......... (visit) the park
Fill in the blank: My mother ……......... (point) at the children
Fill in the blank: my sister ……......... (jump) in a puddle
Fill in the blank: I ……......... (enjoy) my visit to the park
Fill in the blank: My sister ……......... (want) to go to the pond
Fill in the blank: I ……......... (decide) to stay
Fill in the blank: I ……......... (watch) the game
Fill in the blank: then ……......... (join) my mother and sister
Fill in the blank: I ……......... (spot) a goldfish
Fill in the blank: my sister ……......... (notice) two tadpoles

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us Write A: You read how the people of Nagaland made the game of Hekko. Use the space given below to write about a new game that you would like to play. Fill in: Game name, Time, Number of players, Material needed, Rules, Foul.

question_bank.stem: Write about a new game you would like to play. Fill in the given format: Game name, Time, Number of players, Material needed, Rules, Foul.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us Explore: Nagaland is a land of festivals. Hornbill is one of the festivals to promote the culture of the state. Find out more about it.

question_bank.stem: Find out more about the Hornbill festival of Nagaland. Share what you learn.
